summary: Ported a large block of Mario fighter behavior from asm/melee/ft/chara/ftmario.s into src/melee/ft/chara/ftmario.c, adding C implementations for func_800E111C through func_800E187C and deleting roughly 572 lines of assembly. The PR also expanded ftmario.h prototypes, refined ftMarioAttributes fields at offsets 0x04-0x10, and corrected Mario's x223C state field in fighter.h from u32 to HSD_GObj* so callback/item-object code could be typed as a pointer. There was no PR body or review discussion in the slice, so intent and review rationale are inferred from the diff only.
what_changed: Removed the assembly implementations for many Mario functions in the 0x800E111C-0x800E187C range from ftmario.s and replaced them with C in ftmario.c.

Added C implementations for state-transition helpers such as func_800E1178 and func_800E11E0, which call func_8007D5D4/func_8007D7FC, enter action states 0x158/0x157, and set x21BC_callback_Accessory4 to func_800E0EE0.

Added func_800E1248, func_800E132C, func_800E1368, func_800E13C8, and func_800E13F8 for managing ft->sa.mario.x223C, hitlag callbacks, damage/death callbacks, and cleanup via func_802B2560/func_802B2674/func_802B26C0/func_802B26E0.
Added action-state helpers for states 0x159 and 0x15A in func_800E1450 and func_800E14C8, initializing ftcmd vars, stateVar1, velocity, and accessory callback func_800E1248.

Added per-frame or physics-like routines func_800E15D0 and func_800E16E0 that react to ftcmd vars, compute bone coordinates with func_8007500C and func_8000B1CC, spawn an effect through func_800119DC, toggle x2340_stateVar1, update x2218_flag.bits.b3, and call movement helpers such as func_80084F3C, func_8007AEF8, func_8007D494, func_8007D4B8, and func_8007CE94.
Simplified earlier C functions by inlining temporary result variables in guard checks, e.g. if (func_8006F238(gobj) == 0).
Changed func_800E0A00 from void(void) to void(HSD_GObj*) so it can forward gobj to func_800E1368 and be assigned to fighter callbacks that receive a gobj.
Changed SpecialAttrs_Mario.x223C in fighter.h from u32 to HSD_GObj*, matching its use as an object pointer assigned from func_802B2560 and passed to func_802B2674/func_802B26C0/func_802B26E0.
Expanded ftMarioAttributes with f32 fields x04, x08, x0C, and x10 before x14, replacing a padding-only area with named float offsets used by the new code.
Updated remaining ftmario.s references from local labels lbl_800E13C8 and lbl_800E1248 to C symbols func_800E13C8 and func_800E1248.
smart_moves: Retyping x223C as HSD_GObj* made the C code reflect real usage instead of preserving a raw u32 field; this improves callback and item/object cleanup readability.
The conversion kept function names address-based, avoiding unsupported semantic renames while still moving code out of assembly.

The author used callback field names such as x21BC_callback_Accessory4, x21D4_callback_EnterHitlag, x21D8_callback_ExitHitlag, x21E4_callback_OnDeath2, and x21DC_callback_OnTakeDamage to document behavior without inventing higher-level Mario-specific names.

The C port preserved the assembly's callback lifecycle: create object, store it in x223C and x1984_heldItemSpec, install hitlag/death/damage callbacks, then clear callbacks and object pointer during cleanup.
Adding concrete ftMarioAttributes float fields only where the new code dereferenced them reduced blind padding and made later Mario decomp work less offset-heavy.
Leaving unused padding arrays and temporaries in several functions appears to have been a matching tactic for stack layout while still providing maintainable C structure.
decomp_lessons: When a fighter special-attributes or state field is repeatedly passed to HSD_GObj APIs, update the struct type to the pointer type rather than carrying a raw integer through the decomp.
Callback signatures matter: func_800E0A00 needed an HSD_GObj* parameter because it is stored in fighter callback slots and forwards to a gobj-dependent cleanup function.

For fighter callbacks that manage spawned child objects, model the full lifecycle in C: spawn/store object, mirror to heldItemSpec if present, set enter/exit hitlag callbacks, set death/damage cleanup callbacks, then clear all relevant fields on cleanup.
Existing callback field names can be strong evidence for function behavior even when all function names remain autogenerated.

Struct refinement can be safely incremental: replacing padding with named f32 fields at observed offsets x04/x08/x0C/x10 was enough to support func_800E16E0 without over-defining the rest of ftMarioAttributes.
Residual assembly that references newly decompiled internal labels must be updated to the exported C function symbols or linker references will continue to point at deleted labels.
Small control-flow cleanups such as direct condition calls can coexist with an asm-to-C port, but should be evidence-backed by matching output.
naming_conventions: Unidentified functions retained address-based names such as func_800E1248, func_800E13C8, and func_800E16E0.
Local asm labels that became callable C functions were promoted from lbl_800E1248/lbl_800E13C8 to func_800E1248/func_800E13C8 in remaining assembly references.
Mario special attributes used offset-style names x04, x08, x0C, x10, and x14 rather than speculative semantic names.

Fighter state and callback fields used existing descriptive hybrid names such as x2200_ftcmd_var0, x2200_ftcmd_var1, x2200_ftcmd_var2, x2340_stateVar1, x1984_heldItemSpec, and x21BC_callback_Accessory4.
The code uses ft for Fighter* from gobj->user_data, sA or sa for ftMarioAttributes*, and gobj2 for a spawned/stored HSD_GObj*.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Deleted a contiguous block of ftmario.s after matching equivalent C implementations, reducing hand-written assembly by hundreds of lines.
Used explicit unused variables and padding arrays such as u8 padding[8], u8 unused[16], u8 unused1[24], and u8 unused1[28], likely to preserve stack layout and register allocation.

Used casts such as (ftMarioAttributes*)ft->x2D4_specialAttributes, ((Vec3*)(ft->x2D4_specialAttributes))->x, and ((u32*)ft_2->x2D4_specialAttributes) to match known offsets while the attribute struct was still only partially understood.
Preserved immediate action-state IDs and flags from assembly: 0x157, 0x158, 0x159, 0x15A, 0x5000, bone indices 0x31 and 0x4, and effect/id value 0x78.
Matched callback function-pointer stores explicitly rather than abstracting them away, which keeps the generated code close to the original assembly side effects.
Included a decomp.me scratch link comment for func_800E1248, indicating scratch-based matching work for at least that function.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that semantically renamed Mario funcs in the 0x800E111C-0x800E187C range.
Search for subsequent cleanup of duplicate func_800E13F8 prototype in ftmario.h.
Look for later refinement of ftMarioAttributes fields x04, x08, x0C, x10, and x14.
Find PRs that further type or rename SpecialAttrs_Mario.x223C and related Mario object callbacks.
Search for decompilation or naming of func_802B2560, func_802B2674, func_802B26C0, and func_802B26E0 to understand the spawned object managed by x223C.
